summary: The page closes the extended Chamber of the Lambs debate. Segment [0] reaches a verdict: Middot follows R. Eliezer ben Yaakov. Then Rav Adda b. R. Yitzchak offers a different resolution: the Chamber of the Lambs was not in a corner at all but along the middle of the west wall, making its apparent location depend on the viewer's approach direction. The Gemara prefers the two-authority resolution (SW per Middot vs NW per Tamid reflect different tannaitic traditions). The page then transitions to a new topic: the High Priest's rights to sacrifice first and take first - followed by a debate about how many Shewbread loaves the KG receives when the twelve loaves are distributed on Shabbat between the outgoing and incoming watch-shifts.

display.title: Closing the debate: a simpler verdict over a viewing-angle workaround
display.whats: The page closes the extended Chamber of the Lambs debate. First, a verdict is reached: Tractate Middot follows R. Eliezer ben Yaakov, and the Chamber of the Lambs was in the southwest corner of the Beit HaMoked. Then Rav Adda bar R. Yitzchak offers a different approach: the Chamber of the Lambs was not in any corner but along the middle of the west wall - so it appeared to be in different corners from different viewing angles. The Gemara debates which resolution is more defensible.
display.hint: Two competing resolutions both manage to explain the Yoma/Middot discrepancy. The Gemara must pick one: either (A) two authorities with different corner placements, or (B) one chamber in the middle of the west wall appearing at different corners depending on where you stand.

learning.learnerQuestion: Two explanations both reconcile Yoma and Middot - so why does the Gemara prefer the two-authority resolution over Rav Adda's single-chamber-seen-from-different-angles theory?
learning.coreTension: Rav Adda's middle-of-the-west-wall theory is architecturally creative and explains the Yoma/Middot discrepancy just as well as the two-authority theory, yet the sugya has to settle on one conclusion.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara favors the resolution that needs no further assumptions: since the Women's Courtyard amnesia admission and the matching measurement proofs already independently establish R. Eliezer ben Yaakov's authorship of Middot, the two-authority explanation is adopted over Rav Adda's unproven architectural workaround. The sugya then pivots immediately to a different topic, signaling that the debate's value was textual reconciliation, not an ongoing legal consequence.
learning.resolution: The SW corner placement, per Middot and R. Eliezer ben Yaakov, stands as the working conclusion. Rav Adda's middle-of-the-west-wall alternative is noted but not adopted.

takeaway.text: When two explanations resolve a contradiction equally well, the one requiring fewer new assumptions is preferred - and once adopted, the debate's payoff is reconciling the texts, not producing a new practical ruling.

learning.ahaMoment: The 'different viewing angles' resolution is clever but ultimately unprovable - the Gemara prefers the simpler attribution to two different authorities, then moves straight to a topic where the verdict carries no further practical weight.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners often expect an attribution debate like this to end in a practical legal ruling, so the abrupt shift to the Shewbread topic feels disconnected; the actual payoff is settling which tannaitic tradition is correct, not creating new Temple-service halakha.
learning.memoryAnchor: Chamber of Lambs verdict: SW corner per R. Eliezer ben Yaakov / Middot. Alternative considered and set aside: a single chamber on the west wall that looked different from north vs south.

argumentFlow.label: Verdict: Middot = R. EbY, Chamber at SW corner
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The weight of evidence (Women's Courtyard admission, measurement alignment) establishes that Tractate Middot was authored by R. Eliezer ben Yaakov. The Chamber of the Lambs was in the southwest corner.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Adda: Chamber was on the middle of the west wall
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Adda bar R. Yitzchak
argumentFlow.text: Perhaps the Chamber of the Lambs was not in any corner at all but extended along the middle of the west wall. From the north it appeared to be in the SW corner; from the south it appeared in the NW corner.

argumentFlow.label: Evaluating the two resolutions
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Both resolutions explain the discrepancy. The first (two tannaim) is simpler. The second (perspective-dependent) is architecturally creative. The Gemara notes that the SW corner placement (Middot) is more probable.

argumentFlow.label: New topic: High Priest's first share and Shewbread
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara transitions to a new mishnah: the High Priest sacrifices first and takes the first share. A debate follows about how many Shewbread loaves the KG receives.

argumentFlow.label: How many loaves does the KG receive?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The twelve Shewbread loaves are split between the departing and incoming Temple watch-shifts. The KG receives his share from both halves. How many does he take - half of twelve (six) or something different?

concepts.def: Shewbread - twelve loaves arranged in two stacks on the golden table in the Sanctuary, replaced every Shabbat. On replacement, the old loaves are distributed between the incoming and outgoing priestly shifts.
Priestly watch-shift - the 24 families of kohanim each served a week in the Temple on rotation. On Shabbat the outgoing mishmar and incoming mishmar would each receive six Shewbread loaves.

requiresUnderstanding: The Chamber of the Lambs debate from 15b-17a

misconceptions.misconception: The Chamber of the Lambs controversy is merely academic with no real stakes.
misconceptions.correction: It matters for reconstructing accurate Temple topography and for establishing which tannaitic traditions are authoritative about the Temple's physical structure.

visualizableElements.desc: Beit HaMoked with Chamber of Lambs shown at SW corner (final position), with annotation showing how it appeared at NW from the south view
Shewbread distribution on Shabbat: twelve loaves split between outgoing and incoming shifts, with KG taking his portion

quizSeeds.question: Why does the Gemara prefer the two-authority resolution over Rav Adda's single-chamber-viewed-from-different-angles theory?
quizSeeds.answer: The two-authority resolution already has independent support from the Women's Courtyard amnesia admission and the matching measurement proofs, so it needs no further assumptions, while Rav Adda's theory is a creative but unproven architectural workaround.

quizSeeds.question: After the Chamber of the Lambs debate closes, what new topic does the Gemara turn to and what question opens it?
quizSeeds.answer: The High Priest's right to sacrifice and take his portion first, which opens into a dispute over how many of the twelve Shewbread loaves the KG receives when they are divided between the outgoing and incoming priestly watch-shifts.

sugyot.finalRuling: SW corner (Middot/R. EbY) is the accepted location for the Chamber of the Lambs. The KG's Shewbread allocation debate continues on 17b.

glossary.en: Shewbread - twelve loaves on the golden table, replaced every Shabbat and divided between kohanim.
Priestly watch-shift - one of 24 rotating kohanic families, each serving one week in the Temple.
Shewbread - twelve loaves on the golden table, replaced every Shabbat and divided between kohanim.
Priestly watch-shift - one of 24 rotating kohanic families, each serving one week in the Temple.

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: 'it is agreed that it was in the SW corner' - verdict from the Shewbread chamber identification that only SW works for Middot.
Rashi: Rav Adda bar Yitzchak's alternative: the Chamber of the Lambs was not in a true corner but extended along the west wall, appearing NW from south, SW from north.
Rashi: 'it was cut off at both corners' - the chamber was long and extended along the west wall between the two western corners.
Rashi: 'from the north it appears to be in the SW' - approaching from north you see the chamber jutting out at the SW corner.
Rashi: 'from the south it appears as NW' - from the south, the chamber's northern extent looks like the NW corner.
Rashi: 'it is more probable it was in the SW corner' - the Gemara prefers the simple two-authorities solution over Rav Adda's perspective-dependent one.
Rashi: 'since we establish Shewbread against Shewbread' - using the SE Shewbread chamber as fixed, all others fall into place with SW for Lambs.
Rashi: 'from Shewbread' - the Shewbread chamber in SE is anchor; rightward from there: Seals (NE), Hearth (NW), Lambs (SW) = Middot's order.
Rashi: 'if you grant: the Tamid tanna goes left' - the leftward count starting from south places Lambs in NW (Tamid).
Rashi: this explains how both Tamid (NW) and Middot (SW) describe the same chamber - each author counted in a different circuit direction.
Rashi: 'but if you say it was in the NW corner exactly' - Rav Adda's middle-wall theory is less probable than two-circuit-direction theory.
Rashi: Rav Adda's resolution cannot explain the Shewbread placement contradiction - so it is not adopted.
Rashi: 'from Shewbread we contra Shewbread' - the Shewbread reference in Middot's chamber listing resolves which corner each chamber was in.
Rashi: new topic - 'two loaves of Shavuot' - the two communal leavened loaves brought on Shavuot are now discussed in the context of the KG's share.
Rashi: 'four or five' - the number of Shewbread loaves the KG receives each Shabbat varies: sometimes four, sometimes five.
Rashi: 'and it was for Aaron and his sons' - the Shewbread verse assigns it to the kohanim; the KG's share is derived from this.
Rashi: 'half for Aaron etc.' - each Shabbat the twelve loaves are split: half to the outgoing shift, half to the incoming shift, with the KG taking from both.
Rashi: despite there being twelve loaves, two of them (the KG's) are never split - explained below.
Rashi: 'we arrive at the Rabbanan' - Abaye's resolution involves the Rabbanan's position that the KG takes less than half.
Rashi: 'beginning and middle = Rabbanan' - the mishna's opening and middle clauses reflect the Rabbanan; only the end reflects R. Yehuda.
